data.title: Consolidated Edison raises dividend by 4.4%
data.description:
• Consolidated Edison (ED) declared $0.8875/share quarterly dividend, 4.4% increase from prior dividend of $0.8500.
  • Forward yield 3.37%
  • Payable March 16; for shareholders of record Feb. 18; ex-div Feb. 18.
  • See ED Dividend Scorecard, Yield Chart, & Dividend Growth.

data.title: Silver Tiger Metals announces C$40 mln bought-deal share offering
data.description:
• Silver Tiger Metals (SLVR:CA) on Tuesday said it has agreed to a bought-deal financing under which a syndicate of underwriters led by Stifel Canada and BMO Capital Markets will purchase 34,189,000 common shares at C$1.17 per share, raising gross proceeds of about C$40.0 million.
  • The company said the underwriters have a 30-day option to buy up to an additional 15% of the offering, which would increase gross proceeds to roughly C$46.0 million if fully exercised.
  • Silver Tiger plans to use the net proceeds to fund exploration and development at its El Tigre project in Mexico, as well as for working capital and general corporate purposes.
  • The shares will be offered by way of a short-form prospectus in Canadian provinces other than Quebec, and to U.S. buyers through a private placement exemption.
  • The offering is expected to close on or about February 18, subject to regulatory approvals, including from the TSX Venture Exchange.
  • SLVTF -1.40% after hours to $0.961.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Nucor targets 5% increase in steel mill shipments for 2026 while advancing major growth projects
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Nucor Corporation (NUE) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Leon Topalian, Chairman and CEO, opened the call by highlighting Nucor's safety achievements, noting "our team achieved the lowest injury and illness rate in our history, marking the eighth consecutive year of improvement." He described 2025 as a year of "significant growth and transformation" and underscored the company's momentum into 2026.
  • Topalian announced management changes, with Steve Laxton promoted to President and COO while maintaining his CFO responsibilities until a successor is named, and Dave Sumoski set to retire in June after more than 30 years with the company.
  • Topalian reported "adjusted earnings of $1.73 per share in the fourth quarter and $7.71 per share for the full year. EBITDA totaled $918 million for the quarter and approximately $4.2 billion for the year." He emphasized the completion of major capital projects and investments in high-margin products, stating, "Major projects completed include our new rebar micro-mill in Lexington, North Carolina, a new melt shop at our bar mill in Kingman, Arizona... All of these projects are on track to be fully ramped up and operating at positive EBITDA run rates within the year."
  • Topalian discussed trade policy as a key factor, noting, "Foreign import share of the U.S. finished steel market has dropped from approximately 25% at this time last year to 16% in October and an estimated 14% in November." He expects import levels to remain at or below these levels in 2026.
  • Stephen Laxton, President, CFO & COO, stated, "During the fourth quarter, Nucor generated adjusted net earnings of $400 million or $1.73 per share. For the full year, adjusted net earnings were approximately $1.8 billion or $7.71 per share." Laxton highlighted the impact of $27 million in charges related to asset impairments and outlined capital spending of $3.4 billion for 2025, with a planned reduction to approximately $2.5 billion in 2026.

Outlook

  • Nucor expects "higher consolidated earnings with improved results across all 3 operating segments" in the first quarter of 2026, supported by "healthy demand environment, typical positive seasonal trends and fewer outages relative to the fourth quarter," according to Laxton.
  • Topalian stated, "We expect domestic steel demand to be slightly up relative to 2025" and projected a "5%" increase in steel mill shipments compared to 2025.
  • The company's backlog entering 2026 is "up nearly 40% year-over-year in the steel mills segment and 15% in steel products," with structural group backlogs setting new records.
  • Capital expenditures are forecast to decrease, with Laxton estimating "current estimate for 2026 CapEx is approximately $2.5 billion."

Financial Results

  • Nucor reported "adjusted earnings of $1.73 per share in the fourth quarter and $7.71 per share for the full year," with quarterly EBITDA at $918 million and yearly EBITDA at approximately $4.2 billion.
  • Pretax earnings for the steel mills segment were $516 million, reflecting an "8%" decline in shipment volumes, and pretax earnings for steel products were $230 million, down from $319 million in the prior quarter.
  • The raw materials segment generated pretax earnings of approximately $24 million, impacted by scheduled outages at DRI facilities.
  • The company ended 2025 with $2.7 billion in cash and returned $1.2 billion to shareholders through dividends and share buybacks, representing about 70% of net earnings.
  • Start-up costs for growth projects totaled $496 million in 2025, and these are expected to remain elevated in 2026 as projects transition from ramp-up to full operation.

Q&A

  • Lawson Winder, BofA Securities: Asked about 2027 CapEx trends and maintenance capital guidance. Topalian emphasized, "we didn't have to pivot... We can be incredibly prudent and disciplined with how we think about spending our valuable shareholder capital to grow this company meaningful." Laxton indicated, "I'd guide you to a figure closer to $800 million a year now for that just because of the inflation that we've seen in the last several years post-COVID and just the size of our company."
  • Timna Tanners, Wells Fargo: Inquired about achieving through-cycle EBITDA targets of $6.7 billion. Topalian responded, "as we culminate the West Virginia start-up and then bring that to its full ramp capabilities... I believe in the long-term growth strategy Nucor has had."
  • William Peterson, JPMorgan: Queried about upside potential to the 5% shipment growth expectation. Topalian answered, "we believe that the 5% is not only an achievable number, but the demand profile is going to create some uplift for virtually every product group."
  • Questions addressed expansionary opportunities, trade policy risks, West Virginia mill capabilities, growth CapEx normalization, and M&A strategy. Management consistently emphasized disciplined capital allocation, focus on high-value products, and readiness to capitalize on market trends.
  • Tristan Gresser, BNP Paribas: Asked about incremental EBITDA from completed projects. Laxton replied, "the delta in the EBITDA is about $500 million between just those 4 projects and progress at Brandenburg."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained an inquisitive but generally neutral-to-positive tone, probing for clarity on CapEx, growth projects, market share, and strategic direction.
  • Management's tone was confident and optimistic in both prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently referencing robust demand, strong backlogs, and disciplined investment: "We are confident that with the broadest range of capabilities and solutions in the North American market, our driven and dedicated team is exceptionally well positioned to create value for our customers and shareholders," Laxton stated.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's confidence appeared even more pronounced, with repeated references to record backlogs and new project ramp-ups.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Management reiterated its disciplined growth strategy, but with greater emphasis this quarter on the successful completion and ramp-up of key projects.
  • Guidance for 2026 is more constructive, with explicit targets for shipment growth and higher segment earnings, compared to a more cautious tone in the prior quarter.
  • Analysts shifted focus from operational execution and end-market trends in Q3 to questions about capital allocation, project ramp timelines, and EBITDA contributions in Q4.
  • Key metrics such as adjusted earnings per share, EBITDA, and CapEx were explicitly updated, with the company now projecting a meaningful reduction in CapEx and higher free cash flow for 2026.
  • Management's tone was more optimistic this quarter, highlighting improved market conditions and structural demand tailwinds, while analysts maintained a pragmatic approach.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged ongoing risks related to the pace of project ramp-ups, elevated start-up costs, and the persistence of interest rate-sensitive market weakness (e.g., automotive, residential construction).
  • Trade policy uncertainty remains a focal point, with Topalian stressing the importance of Section 232 tariffs and active trade enforcement.
  • Analysts raised questions about potential increases in imports due to international price differentials, but management argued that strong domestic demand, robust backlogs, and recent trade actions would help mitigate these risks.
  • Laxton noted that "pre-operating and start-up costs totaled $496 million" in 2025 and are expected to remain elevated, signaling a near-term profitability headwind as projects move to full operation.

Final Takeaway

Nucor enters 2026 with record backlogs, a robust pipeline of recently completed projects, and a disciplined approach to capital allocation. Management expects higher consolidated earnings, improved segment performance, and a 5% increase in steel mill shipments for 2026, supported by strong demand in infrastructure, data centers, and energy markets. While start-up costs and market risks remain, the company believes its growth strategy, reduced CapEx, and lower import levels position it well to deliver enhanced value to shareholders in the year ahead.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Trump talks up the dollar as DXY sinks to its lowest level in nearly four years
data.description:
The U.S. dollar (DXY) slid to a near four-year low on Tuesday, extending recent losses after President Donald Trump downplayed concerns over the currency’s decline and struck an optimistic tone on its performance.

“I think it’s great, the value of the dollar,” Trump said to reporters at an event in Iowa. “Look at the business we’re doing. The dollar’s doing great.” 

Markets reacted swiftly to the remarks. The U.S. Dollar Index (DXY), which measures the greenback against a basket of major global currencies, dropped more than 1% shortly after the comments. The index fell to an intraday low of 95.55 before stabilizing near 95.82, marking its weakest level since February 10, 2022. 

The latest move underscores the dollar’s ongoing struggles amid what investors have increasingly described as a broader “Sell America” trade, characterized by reduced demand for U.S. assets. 

As the greenback weakened, precious metals benefited as investors sought alternative stores of value. 

The dollar’s decline reflects growing skepticism around U.S. fiscal and monetary dynamics, even as the White House continues to emphasize economic strength and robust business activity. 

U.S. Dollar Funds: (UUP), (USDU), and (UDN).

data.title: WesBanco Non-GAAP EPS of $0.84 misses by $0.01, revenue of $265.57M in-line
data.description:
• WesBanco press release (WSBC): Q4 Non-GAAP EPS of $0.84 misses by $0.01.
  • Revenue of $265.57M (+63.0% Y/Y) in-line.
  • Net interest margin of 3.61% increased 58 basis points year-over-year and 8 basis points quarter-over-quarter reflecting higher earning asset yields and lower funding costs
  • Efficiency ratio of 51.6% improved more than 8 percentage points year-over-year due to expense synergies generated from the PFC acquisition, as well as a continued focus on expense management and driving positive operating leverage

data.title: Texas Instruments perks up amid Q4 results, guidance
data.description:
Texas Instruments (TXN) shares rose 5.5% in extended trading on Tuesday after the analog semiconductor giant reported fiscal fourth-quarter results and guidance that were largely in-line with Wall Street's forecast.

For the period ending Dec. 31, TI said it earned $1.27 per share as revenue rose 10% year-over-year to $4.42B. Analog revenue rebounded, up 14% year-over-year to $3.62B, while embedded processing revenue rose 8% over the same time frame to $662M. Other revenue tumbled 34% year-over-year to $146M.

In addition, earnings per share included a $0.06 reduction that was not in the company's original guidance, TI added.

Analysts had expected the company to earn $1.28 per share on $4.43B in revenue.

“Revenue decreased 7% sequentially and increased 10% from the same quarter a year ago,” Haviv Ilan, TI's chairman, president, and CEO, said in a statement. “Our cash flow from operations of $7.2 billion for the trailing 12 months again underscored the strength of our business model, the quality of our product portfolio and the benefit of 300mm production. Free cash flow for the same period was $2.9 billion. Over the past 12 months we invested $3.9 billion in R&D and SG&A, invested $4.6 billion in capital expenditures and returned $6.5 billion to owners.”

Looking to the first-quarter of fiscal 2026, Texas Instruments said it expects to earn between $1.22 and $1.48 per share, with the midpoint above the analyst estimate of $1.26 per share. Sales are expected to be between $4.32B and $4.68B, with the midpoint of $4.5B above the forecast of $4.42B.

The company will host a conference call at 4:30 p.m. EST to discuss the results.

data.title: Precious metals hit record highs, but ‘the harder they come, the harder they fall’
data.description:
The gold spot price (XAUUSD:CUR) reached an all-time high on Tuesday, achieving $5,186.89 per ounce, as precious metals hit records this week.

Gold (XAUUSD:CUR), silver (XAGUSD:CUR), and platinum (XPTUSD:CUR) rose to new all-time highs in January, with the silver spot price surpassing $112 per ounce on Tuesday, and Citigroup expecting it to reach $150 in three months.

But Seeking Alpha analyst Andrew Hecht argued that “gravity has yet to hit” these precious metals as “parabolic” price action has continued into late January.

“The harder they come, the harder they fall, one and all,” he said. “Corrections in gold and silver could be overdue. Be cautious with precious metals, as they have risen sharply in a short time.”

As an example, the platinum spot price (XPTUSD:CUR) was -8.7% on Tuesday after a historic run, and the S&P GSCI Palladium Index Spot (SPGSPA) was -14.1% after reaching a record high this week.

Markets closed mixed on Tuesday, with the S&P 500 (SP500) ending at a record high, the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) closing +0.9%, and the Dow (DJI) -0.8%.
